title: Mixture of Experts — MoE Architecture
description: Mixture of Experts (MoE) is the dominant scaling paradigm for modern LLMs — replacing dense FFN layers with sparse expert modules. Used in Mixtral 8x7B, GPT-4, DeepSeek MoE, and Qwen2-MoE. FAANG interviews test routing mechanics, load balancing, and memory vs compute tradeoffs.

sections.title: Why MoE? Sparse Activation at Dense-Equivalent Quality
sections.description:
A dense transformer activates all parameters for every token. A sparse MoE transformer replaces each FFN layer with multiple expert FFNs and a router that selects a subset (typically top-2) per token. Each token only computes through 2 experts, not all N_experts. This decouples total parameters from compute per token.

Key numbers to know:

ModelTotal ParamsActive ParamsExpertsTop-K
Mixtral 8x7B~47B~13B82
GPT-4 (reported)~1.7T~280B162
DeepSeek MoE 16B~17B~3B646
Qwen2-MoE 2.7B~14B~2.2B604

FAANG interview angle: 'Design a cost-effective model architecture for a 1T-parameter LLM.' The answer involves MoE: 16 experts, top-2 routing, router on a separate GPU pipeline stage.
sections.codeBlock:
# Simplified MoE FFN layer
class MoELayer(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, n_experts=8, top_k=2):
        super().__init__()
        self.n_experts = n_experts
        self.top_k = top_k
        # Router: learned linear projection to expert logits
        self.router = nn.Linear(d_model, n_experts, bias=False)
        # Expert FFNs: each is a standard 2-layer MLP
        self.experts = nn.ModuleList([
            nn.Sequential(
                nn.Linear(d_model, 4 * d_model),
                nn.SiLU(),
                nn.Linear(4 * d_model, d_model),
            )
            for _ in range(n_experts)
        ])

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: [B, T, d]
        logits = self.router(x)                              # [B, T, E]
        weights, indices = torch.topk(logits, self.top_k, dim=-1)  # [B, T, K]
        weights = F.softmax(weights, dim=-1)                 # normalize top-k

        out = torch.zeros_like(x)
        # Dispatch each token to its top-k experts (gather/scatter)
        # ponytail: naive loop; real impl uses parallel dispatch
        for b in range(x.shape[0]):
            for t in range(x.shape[1]):
                for k in range(self.top_k):
                    expert_idx = indices[b, t, k]
                    weight = weights[b, t, k]
                    out[b, t] += weight * self.experts[expert_idx](x[b, t])
        return out

sections.title: Routing Strategies: Softmax-TopK, Noisy Top-K, Expert Choice
sections.description:
The router design is the core algorithmic contribution in each MoE paper. Key approaches:

Softmax-TopK (Shazeer et al., 2017): Router outputs E logits → softmax → select top-K → compute only through those K experts. Simple and effective. Top-2 is standard.

Noisy Top-K (Switch Transformer, Fedus et al., 2022): Add learnable Gaussian noise to router logits during training before softmax. This improves load balancing by breaking deterministic routing patterns. At inference, noise is removed.

Expert Choice (Zhou et al., 2022): Instead of tokens choosing experts, each expert chooses its top tokens. Each expert selects its capacity C of tokens with the highest affinity. Guarantees perfect load balance — every expert processes exactly C tokens. Used in DeepSeek MoE.

FAANG interview tip: Compare routing strategies: Softmax-TopK is simplest but causes load imbalance. Expert Choice fixes imbalance but loses the 'token looks for the best expert' property. Always mention the tradeoff.
sections.codeBlock:
# Routing strategies compared
import torch, torch.nn.functional as F

def softmax_topk_route(x, router_weight, top_k=2):
    """Standard MoE routing: tokens choose experts."""
    scores = F.softmax(x @ router_weight, dim=-1)   # [B, T, E]
    topk_vals, topk_idx = torch.topk(scores, top_k, dim=-1)
    topk_vals = topk_vals / topk_vals.sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    return topk_idx, topk_vals

def expert_choice_route(x, router_weight, capacity):
    """
    Expert Choice routing: experts choose tokens.
    capacity = how many tokens each expert processes.
    """
    scores = x @ router_weight                    # [B, T, E] → [N, E]
    scores = scores.view(-1, scores.shape[-1])    # flatten batch+seq
    # Each expert picks its top-capacity tokens
    topk_vals, topk_idx = torch.topk(scores, capacity, dim=0)  # [cap, E]
    # topk_idx[t, e] = token index chosen by expert e at position t
    return topk_idx, topk_vals.softmax(dim=0)

# ponytail: real impl needs token dropping/recycling for Expert Choice

sections.title: Load Balancing: The Fundamental Challenge
sections.description:
Without load balancing, the router learns to route most tokens to 2–3 'favorite' experts — creating a compute bottleneck where some experts are fully utilized while others sit idle. This defeats the purpose of MoE and wastes GPU memory.

Load Balancing Loss (Auxiliary Loss): Applied to the router logits during training. The standard formulation penalizes imbalance in both: (a) the fraction of tokens routed to each expert, and (b) the fraction of router weight assigned to each expert. Formula: α × E × Σ(f_i × P_i), where f_i = fraction of tokens routed to expert i, P_i = average router probability for expert i, and α is the loss coefficient (typically 0.01).

Expert Capacity: Each expert has a maximum capacity = ceil(tokens_per_batch / n_experts × capacity_factor). If tokens exceed capacity, excess tokens are 'dropped' (re-routed to the next expert or processed via residual connection). capacity_factor=1.0 means perfect balance. Typical: 1.1–1.25 for some slack.

Z-loss (DeepSeek MoE): An auxiliary loss that penalizes large router logits by adding β × mean(logits²). Stabilizes training by preventing router logits from growing unbounded and causing softmax saturation.
sections.codeBlock:
# Load balancing auxiliary loss (Switch Transformer style)
def load_balancing_loss(router_logits, expert_indices, top_k=2, alpha=0.01):
    """
    router_logits: [B*T, E] — pre-softmax logits
    expert_indices: [B*T, K] — chosen expert IDs per token
    """
    n_tokens, n_experts = router_logits.shape
    
    # Fraction of tokens routed to each expert
    # One-hot from indices, sum over tokens, divide by n_tokens
    mask = F.one_hot(expert_indices, num_classes=n_experts)  # [N, K, E]
    tokens_per_expert = mask.sum(dim=(0, 1)).float()          # [E]
    f_i = tokens_per_expert / (n_tokens * top_k)              # [E]
    
    # Average router probability per expert
    probs = F.softmax(router_logits, dim=-1)                  # [N, E]
    P_i = probs.mean(dim=0)                                   # [E]
    
    # Auxiliary loss: product of f_i and P_i, summed over experts
    loss = alpha * n_experts * (f_i * P_i).sum()
    return loss

# Z-loss (DeepSeek MoE) — penalizes large router logits
def z_loss(router_logits, beta=0.001):
    """Stabilizes training by keeping router logits bounded."""
    return beta * router_logits.pow(2).mean()

# ponytail: loss scales with n_experts; tune alpha on small runs

sections.title: MoE Training Challenges: All-to-All Communication & Token Dropping
sections.metadata:
MoE training requires all-to-all communication between GPUs — tokens are scattered to expert GPUs, processed, then gathered back. This is the #1 engineering challenge.

All-to-All Dispatch: At each MoE layer, the router decides which tokens go to which expert. Tokens must be reordered and sent to the GPU that holds each expert's weights. This creates two all-to-all communication steps: scatter (send tokens to expert GPUs) and gather (return processed tokens). Communication overhead scales with the number of tokens sent per expert, not the number of experts.

Token Dropping: During training, if an expert's capacity is exceeded, excess tokens are 'dropped' — they pass through via a residual connection (skip the FFN entirely). This creates a gradient that encourages the router to improve balance. However, dropping too many tokens (>5%) hurts model quality. Solutions:
• Increase capacity_factor
• Use Expert Choice routing (no dropping)
• Auxiliary loss annealing during training

FAANG interview tip: When asked 'How would you train a 1T MoE model?' — mention all-to-all communication as the bottleneck, expert parallelism across GPUs, and load balancing as the key training stability concern.
sections.codeBlock:
# MoE communication pattern (simplified all-to-all)
# Each GPU has a subset of experts. This function runs on each GPU.

def moe_all_to_all(token_hidden, router_logits, expert_map, local_experts, top_k=2):
    """
    token_hidden: [local_tokens, d] — tokens on this GPU
    expert_map: dict {expert_id → gpu_rank}
    local_experts: list of expert FFNs on this GPU
    """
    # Step 1: Route — which expert for each token?
    weights, indices = torch.topk(router_logits.softmax(-1), top_k)
    
    # Step 2: Scatter — send tokens to expert GPUs
    # Build (token, expert_id, weight) tuples, group by target GPU
    send_buffers = {gpu: [] for gpu in set(expert_map.values())}
    for t_idx, (expert_ids, wts) in enumerate(zip(indices, weights)):
        for e_id, wt in zip(expert_ids, wts):
            target_gpu = expert_map[int(e_id)]
            send_buffers[target_gpu].append((t_idx, int(e_id), wt, token_hidden[t_idx]))
    
    # distribute via all-to-all (actual impl uses NCCL send/recv)
    # received_tokens = all_to_all(send_buffers)
    
    # Step 3: Compute — each GPU processes owned tokens through its experts
    # results = []
    # for token, expert_id, weight in received_tokens:
    #     result = weight * local_experts[expert_id](token)
    #     results.append((original_pos, result))
    
    # Step 4: Gather — return results to original GPUs
    # all_to_all(gathered_results)
    # Return results in original token order
    
    # ponytail: real impl uses batched dispatch with sorted token groups
    pass

sections.title: MoE vs Dense: When to Choose What
sections.metadata:
MoE is not universally better than dense — the choice depends on serving constraints and budget.

CriterionDenseMoE
Quality/paramBaselineHigher at same active params
Memory footprintLowerHigher (all experts in VRAM)
Inference throughputPredictableVariable (load balance)
Batch size sensitivityLinear scalingBetter at small batch (higher compute utilization)
Training complexitySimpleHigh (all-to-all, load balance, stability)
Serving costLower if batch size is largeLower if latency-sensitive (active params lower)

When MoE wins: You want the quality of a large model but inference must be fast. Batch size is small (chat applications). Training budget is large enough to handle all-to-all communication overhead.

When dense wins: Memory-constrained deployment (single GPU). Very large batch inference (the all-to-all overhead amortizes poorly). Training infra that can't support expert parallelism.

sections.title: Fine-Tuning MoE: Routing Collapse & Expert Specialization
sections.metadata:
Fine-tuning MoE models has unique challenges beyond dense model FT:

Routing Collapse: During fine-tuning on a narrow domain, the router may learn to route all tokens to a single expert — defeating the purpose of MoE. Mitigation: Use a higher load-balancing loss coefficient during fine-tuning (e.g., α=0.1 vs α=0.01 for pre-training).

Expert Specialization: With continued pre-training, experts naturally specialize in topics (code, math, biology). This can be exploited: you can prune irrelevant experts for domain-specific deployment. Research shows Mixtral 8x7B's experts specialize in e.g., code, reasoning, factual knowledge.

LoRA on MoE: Standard LoRA works on MoE — apply adapters to both the router and the expert FFNs. The router's LoRA adapter is critical because it needs to adapt routing to the new domain. For memory efficiency: only train LoRA on top-K experts (not all 8).

Expert Merging: For deployment on a single GPU, experts can be merged via task arithmetic or expert distillation (collapse 8 experts into 1 dense FFN). Distillation retains ~90-95% quality at 8× lower memory.
sections.codeBlock:
# LoRA fine-tuning on MoE — freeze experts, train adapters + router bias
class MoELoRA(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, moe_layer, rank=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.moe = moe_layer
        # Freeze all expert parameters
        for expert in self.moe.experts:
            for p in expert.parameters():
                p.requires_grad = False
        
        # LoRA adapters only for the router (most important)
        d_in = self.moe.router.in_features
        n_exp = self.moe.router.out_features
        self.router_lora_A = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(d_in, rank) * 0.01)
        self.router_lora_B = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(rank, n_exp))
        
        # Optional: expert bias terms for quick domain adaptation
        self.expert_biases = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(n_exp))

    def forward(self, x):
        # Router with LoRA adaptation
        lora_adjustment = x @ self.router_lora_A @ self.router_lora_B
        router_logits = self.moe.router(x) + lora_adjustment + self.expert_biases
        
        # Standard MoE forward with adapted router
        weights, indices = torch.topk(router_logits.softmax(-1), self.moe.top_k)
        out = torch.zeros_like(x)
        for b in range(x.shape[0]):
            for t in range(x.shape[1]):
                for k in range(self.moe.top_k):
                    out[b, t] += weights[b, t, k] * self.moe.experts[indices[b, t, k]](x[b, t])
        return out

# ponytail: real implementation uses batched dispatch, not nested loops

details:
MoE for Inference Throughput: At small batch sizes (typical for chat), MoE offers 3-5× higher throughput than a dense model of equivalent quality because active parameters are ~25% of total. At very large batch sizes, the all-to-all communication overhead erodes this advantage. Mixtral 8x7B achieves ~2.5× throughput vs Llama 2 70B at comparable quality.

Multi-Token Prediction (MTP): An alternative to MoE for efficient training — trains the model to predict K future tokens at once (instead of 1). The shared trunk processes all K predictions simultaneously, increasing compute utilization per forward pass. Used in Meta's 2024 research and DeepSeek V3. Not mutually exclusive with MoE — DeepSeek V3 uses both.

FAANG interview tip: When asked 'Design a 1T+ parameter LLM' — propose an MoE architecture. Specify: number of experts (16-64), top-k (2-6), capacity factor (1.1-1.25), expert parallelism over GPUs, and auxiliary loss (load balancing + Z-loss). Mention the all-to-all bottleneck and suggest expert grouping to minimize inter-node communication. Cite DeepSeek MoE and Mixtral as case studies.
